perturbation.original_text: 7. “Non-Disclosure Certificate” means the certificate annexed hereto by which Reviewing Representatives seeking access to Confidential Information shall certify their understanding that such access to Confidential Information is provided pursuant to the terms and conditions of this Agreement and that each Reviewing Representative has read this Agreement and agrees to be bound by it.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. “Non-Disclosure Certificate” means a mere formality with no legal bearing. It is a document that may or may not be signed at the discretion of the individual, and its absence does not restrict access to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition describes the Non-Disclosure Certificate as a legally binding document required for accessing Confidential Information. The modified definition reduces it to a formality with no legal effect, negating its intended purpose. This creates a direct contradiction with section B.2, which requires the certificate to be signed, making it uncertain whether Reviewing Representatives are required to sign a Non-Disclosure Certificate to access confidential information, because based on the perturbation, it is a mere formality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Reviewing Representative. A Reviewing Representative shall not have access to any Confidential Information unless that Reviewing Representative is required to have the information in order to carry out that person’s Transmission Functions responsibilities and has executed the attached Non-Disclosure Certificate. The Reviewing Representative shall deliver a copy of his or her executed Non-Disclosure Certificate to PJM. A Reviewing Representative shall not use the Confidential Information or any portion thereof to give any Party or a competitor of any Party a competitive or commercial advantage. A Reviewing Representative may make copies or Notes of Confidential Information that shall be subject to this Agreement. In the event a Reviewing Representative ceases to be employed or engaged by a Party, or is employed, retained, or given duties that include Competitive Duties, (i) the Reviewing Representative shall continue to comply with the terms and conditions of this Agreement with respect to the Confidential Information to which such person previously had access, (ii) the Party shall terminate the Reviewing Representative's access to Confidential Information, and (iii) the Party shall cause the Reviewing Representative to return or dispose of the Confidential Information, or transfer the information to another Reviewing Representative of the Party. Each Party shall advise the Reviewing Representatives that Confidential Information is confidential and shall be treated as confidential in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines the 'Non-Disclosure Certificate' from a legally binding document required for accessing confidential information to a mere formality with no legal bearing. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text', specifically section B.2, which states that a Reviewing Representative 'shall not have access to any Confidential Information unless...[they] have executed the attached Non-Disclosure Certificate.' The changed definition makes the requirement of signing the certificate optional, thus creating a direct conflict and uncertainty about whether it is needed to access confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 9. “Reviewing Representative” means an employee of a Party who has signed or electronically signed a Non-Disclosure Certificate and who is a principal, partner, officer, director, employee, agent and other representative of a Party. Reviewing Representative may include a person whose duties include Competitive Duties so long as such Reviewing Representative’s receipt and use of Confidential Information is not prohibited conduct under FERC’s rules, including Standards of Conduct.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. “Reviewing Representative” means any individual, regardless of their affiliation or agreement to confidentiality, who has access to Confidential Information. The term explicitly includes competitors and external consultants, irrespective of any conflict of interest.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition limits Reviewing Representatives to employees who have signed a Non-Disclosure Certificate. The modified definition broadens this to include anyone with access, even competitors. This introduces a contradiction with section B.5 which states 'Parties shall not disclose Confidential Information to a third party without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party.', because based on the modified definition, Confidential Information can be disclosed to any individual without restriction, including third parties like competitors.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Non-Disclosure to Third Parties. Parties shall not disclose Confidential Information to a third party without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party. Each Party shall treat all Confidential Information in every form as confidential, and shall not reveal, divulge or disclose Confidential Information, at any time or for any reason, to any third person or entity. This provision shall survive the expiration, termination or cancellation of this Agreement in accordance with Section 9.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Reviewing Representative' to include anyone with access to Confidential Information, even competitors, regardless of any confidentiality agreements. This directly contradicts section 5, which prohibits disclosing Confidential Information to third parties without prior written approval. The original definition limited access to employees with Non-Disclosure Certificates, maintaining consistency with the non-disclosure obligation. The modified definition breaks this consistency, creating a clear and direct conflict between the two sections, as it allows for disclosure to third parties (competitors) without restriction, which is explicitly prohibited in section 5.

perturbation.original_text:
B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions
The following provisions govern the use of Confidential Information under this Agreement.
1. Disclosure of Confidential Information. A Disclosing Party may disclose or discuss a Party’s Confidential Information with a ny other Party to this Agreement. The disclosure of the Confidential Information is subject to the terms and conditions stated herein. Each Party acknowledges the importance to the other Parties of preserving the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and that a Disclosing Party is relying on the agreements set forth herein in furnishing Confidential Information to a Party. Each Party shall safeguard the Confidential Information at least to the same extent that it would its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text:
B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions
The following provisions are merely suggestions and not binding requirements regarding the use of Confidential Information under this Agreement.
1. Disclosure of Confidential Information. A Disclosing Party may, at their own discretion, choose whether or not to disclose or discuss a Party’s Confidential Information with any other Party to this Agreement. The disclosure of the Confidential Information is not subject to any mandatory terms and conditions. No Party is required to acknowledge the importance of preserving the confidentiality of the Confidential Information, and no Disclosing Party is relying on any agreements set forth herein in furnishing Confidential Information to a Party. Each Party is free to disregard the Confidential Information as they see fit.
perturbation.explanation: The original text emphasizes that the provisions are governing and binding. The modified text suggests they are merely suggestions and not binding. This contradicts the entire agreement, because based on the rest of the agreement, the confidential information should be used in accordance to the legal provisions made.
perturbation.contradicted_text:
B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions
The following provisions are merely suggestions and not binding requirements regarding the use of Confidential Information under this Agreement.
1. Disclosure of Confidential Information. A Disclosing Party may, at their own discretion, choose whether or not to disclose or discuss a Party’s Confidential Information with any other Party to this Agreement. The disclosure of the Confidential Information is not subject to any mandatory terms and conditions. No Party is required to acknowledge the importance of preserving the confidentiality of the Confidential Information, and no Disclosing Party is relying on any agreements set forth herein in furnishing Confidential Information to a Party. Each Party is free to disregard the Confidential Information as they see fit.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the provisions govern the use of confidential information. The changed text explicitly states the provisions are merely suggestions and not binding requirements, and that parties are free to disregard the confidential information. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, as the contradicted text is a duplicate of the changed text, and suggests that no part of the agreement relating to confidential information is binding. This change creates a strong in-text contradiction because it fundamentally alters the binding nature of the confidentiality provisions within the contract, making enforcement of those provisions impossible.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Non-Disclosure to Third Parties. Parties shall not disclose Confidential Information to a third party without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party. Each Party shall treat all Confidential Information in every form as confidential, and shall not reveal, divulge or disclose Confidential Information, at any time or for any reason, to any third person or entity. This provision shall survive the expiration, termination or cancellation of this Agreement in accordance with Section 9.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Non-Disclosure to Third Parties. Parties are encouraged to share Confidential Information with third parties to foster collaboration and innovation. The Disclosing Party waives any right to object to such disclosures. This provision does not survive the expiration, termination, or cancellation of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original section strictly prohibits disclosure to third parties without prior approval. The modified section encourages disclosure and waives the right to object. This contradiction makes it unclear if Parties are required to withhold confidential information, because based on the perturbation, they are encouraged to share the confidential information with Third Parties
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Non-Disclosure to Third Parties. Parties are encouraged to share Confidential Information with third parties to foster collaboration and innovation. The Disclosing Party waives any right to object to such disclosures. This provision does not survive the expiration, termination, or cancellation of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text of Section 5 prohibits disclosure of confidential information to third parties without prior written approval. The changed text of Section 5 encourages sharing confidential information with third parties and waives the right to object. This creates a direct conflict because the changed text encourages a behavior that the original text explicitly forbids, making it unclear which obligation Parties should follow. The change directly creates an in-text contradiction with the contradicted text because it fundamentally opposes the original restriction, aligning with the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Ownership and Use of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information delivered by a Disclosing Party to a Party pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party, and such Confidential Information shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party upon request. That portion of the Confidential Information that may be found in analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by or for a Party and all Confidential Information that is oral will be kept by a Party subject to the terms of this Agreement or destroyed. Neither the Party nor its Reviewing Representatives shall use the Confidential Information for any purpose whatsoever except for the purpose of executing the Reviewing Party’s Transmission Functions. Once the Party no longer requires the use of such information for the purpose of performing its Transmission Functions, the Confidential Information shall be returned or destroyed in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Ownership and Use of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information delivered by a Disclosing Party to a Party pursuant to this Agreement becomes the sole property of the receiving Party and does not need to be returned. The receiving Party has unlimited rights to use, modify, and distribute the Confidential Information without any restrictions or obligations to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original section states that Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party and must be returned. The modified section states that it becomes the property of the receiving Party and does not need to be returned, negating the ownership clause. This creates a contradiction because, under the original agreement, the Disclosing Party maintains ownership and has certain rights, while under the changed definition, the other party gains the right of ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. Ownership and Use of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information delivered by a Disclosing Party to a Party pursuant to this Agreement becomes the sole property of the receiving Party and does not need to be returned. The receiving Party has unlimited rights to use, modify, and distribute the Confidential Information without any restrictions or obligations to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 8 directly contradicts the original agreement regarding ownership and usage rights of Confidential Information. The original states that Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party and must be returned, while the changed text states that it becomes the property of the receiving Party and does not need to be returned, granting unlimited rights. The contradicted text is the changed text, as it causes a contradiction within the agreement. According to the perturbation definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction', key terms or obligations are defined inconsistently in different parts of a contract, making interpretation unclear. This fits the definition, thus a clear contradiction exists.
